system_message:
# Scripture Study — System Context

You are a scripture study assistant collaborating with a human who brings faith in
Jesus Christ and the Restoration. Your role is to facilitate deep, honest scripture
study — producing not just knowledge but personal transformation.

## Core Values

- **Depth over breadth.** Take time to really explore. Surface patterns that casual
  reading misses. Trace words to Hebrew/Greek. Compare across all five standard works.
  Follow the footnotes.
- **Honest exploration.** Engage thoughtfully with hard questions rather than retreating
  to disclaimers. Uncertainty can be acknowledged warmly.
- **Faith as framework.** Work within the framework of faith in Jesus Christ and the
  Restoration. Offer scholarly insight AND spiritual application.

## Constraints

- Every direct quote must be accurate. Do not fabricate or approximate quotations.
- Link claims to specific scriptures. Chapter and verse, not vague references.
- When you don't know something, say so. A faithful "I'm not sure" is better than
  a confident fabrication.

## What Good Output Looks Like

- Cross-references that surface unexpected connections between standard works
- Webster 1828 word analysis that illuminates Restoration-era vocabulary
- Teaching patterns extracted from conference talks
- Doctrinal synthesis that traces themes across multiple sources
- Specific, actionable insights — not generic spiritual platitudes

## Standard Works

The five standard works are: Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon,
Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price. Conference talks from
General Conference (1971–present) are also available.
response:
## Explicit References  
*(Scriptures that the author actually quotes or alludes to in the footnotes and text)*  

| Scripture | How it is used in the content |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Luke 6:20** | Cited to show the biblical precedent for “the poor” being blessed (see footnote 2a). |
| **Luke 7:22** | Same purpose as Luke 6:20 – a parallel witness that the poor are blessed. |
| **Alma 34:40** | Referenced to define “poor” in a spiritual sense (footnote 3b). |
| **Alma 47:5** | Used to locate the hill “Onidah” where Alma teaches (footnote 4a). |
| **Prov 18:23** | Quoted in footnote 5a to illustrate the rhetorical “what shall we do?” question. |
| **Alma 33:10** | Cited in footnote 5c to describe “cast out” of the synagogues. |
| **Alma 33:2** | Referenced in footnote 5d for the phrase “no place to worship.” |
| **Alma 34:3** | Used in footnote 5e to link the question “what shall we do?” to repentance. |
| **Prov 16:1** | Referenced in footnote 6c to illustrate “preparation” for hearing the word. |
| **Matt 5:5** | Cited in footnote 8a to describe the “lowly in heart.” |
| **Matt 5:5 (3‑5)** | Same as above; also used to define “lowly.” |
| **Alma 34:2** | Referenced in footnote 13b to speak of “endureth to the end.” |
| **3 Nephi 15:9** | Same footnote 13b; shows the doctrine of endurance. |
| **3 Nephi 27:6‑17** | Referenced in footnote 13b for the same concept. |
| **Alma 38:2** | Cited in footnote 13b for “endureth.” |
| **2 Kgs 22:19** | Referenced in footnote 14a to illustrate humility before the king. |
| **Matt 15:24** | Used in footnote 19c to show that knowing God’s will and not doing it is cursed. |
| **John 15:24** | Same footnote 19c; emphasizes the seriousness of willful disobedience. |
| **D&C 41:1** | Referenced in footnote 19a to speak of “more” cursed. |
| **TG Humility** (Topical Guide) | Used throughout to label the concept of humility (footnotes 6b, 16a, 25a). |
| **TG Initiative** | Used in footnotes 16b, 25a to describe “compelled” versus “voluntary” humility. |
| **TG Stubbornness** | Used in footnote 16c to contrast “without stubbornness of heart.” |
| **TG Signs** | Referenced in footnote 17a for the desire for a heavenly sign. |
| **Luke 16:30 (27‑31)** | Cited in footnote 18a to define “believe” vs. “know.” |
| **Ether 12:12 (12, 18)** | Same footnote 18a; shows faith as hope in things not seen. |
| **D&C 15:24 (22‑24)** | Referenced in footnote 19e to speak of “transgression.” |
| **John 20:29** | Cited in footnote 21a to define faith as belief without perfect knowledge. |
| **Heb 11:1** | Same footnote 21a; classic definition of faith. |
| **Ether 12:6** | Referenced in footnote 21c to say faith is not perfect knowledge. |
| **TG God, Mercy of** | Used in footnote 22a to describe God’s mercy to believers. |
| **Joel 2:29 (28‑29)** | Referenced in footnote 23a to show that God’s word is given to women and children. |
| **Matt 11:25** | Cited in footnote 23b for “children” receiving words that confound the wise. |
| **Luke 10:21** | Same footnote 23b; parallel to the children‑revelation theme. |
| **3 Nephi 26:14 (14‑16)** | Referenced in footnote 23b for children’s words. |
| **D&C 128:18** | Same footnote 23b; shows the gift of words to children. |
| **TG Discernment, Spiritual** | Used in footnote 28e to describe “enlighten.” |
| **TG Edification** | Same footnote 28e; linked to “understanding.” |
| **TG Intelligence** | Referenced in footnote 28f for “understanding.” |
| **TG Heart** | Used in footnote 28c to locate the “heart” where the seed is planted. |
| **TG Doubt / Unbelief** | Referenced in footnote 28d to warn against casting out the seed by unbelief. |
| **TG Light (noun)** | Used in footnote 35a to equate “light” with what is good and discernible. |
| **Gen 1:4** | Referenced in footnote 35b to define “good” as light. |
| **TG Barren** | Used in footnote 39b to describe the ground as barren. |
| **TG Harvest** | Referenced in footnote 43a to speak of “reap” the rewards of faith. |
| **TG Reward** | Same footnote 43b; the fruit of faith is a reward. |
| **TG Patience** | Used in footnote 41a to describe the needed patience in nourishing the tree. |
| **TG Diligence** | Referenced in footnote 42a to stress diligence in nurturing the word. |

---

## Implicit Connections  
*(Scriptures not directly quoted but whose themes or language echo the passage; the connection matters because it deepens the doctrinal point being made.)*  

| Scripture | Implicit connection & why it matters |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|
| **Alma 33:1‑2** | The seed metaphor in Alma 32 is a direct continuation of Alma 33’s “seed” allegory, reinforcing the idea that the word is a living, growing principle. It shows the consistency of Nephite teaching on spiritual growth. |
| **Jacob 4:12** | “For the sake of the world, the Lord hath blessed the seed of the Church.” The passage’s emphasis on planting the word in “poor hearts” mirrors Jacob’s promise that the humble will inherit the kingdom, linking humility with divine blessing. |
| **Mosiah 2:17** | “When ye are in the service of your fellow beings ye are only in the service of your God.” Alma’s teaching that service and humility precede blessedness echoes this king‑ Benjamin doctrine. |
| **Mosiah 4:9‑10** | “Ye shall serve one another, and only in the service of God can ye be blessed.” The connection is that Alma’s call to “humble yourselves” and “serve the Lord” is a direct parallel to Mosiah’s injunction to serve others. |
| **Alma 34:31‑32** | The phrase “the word shall be a seed” is echoed in Alma 34’s discussion of “the word is the seed of a tree of life.” This reinforces the doctrine that the word must be nurtured to become the tree of life. |
| **D&C 88:33** | “That which is of God is light; and the light giveth you the knowledge of all things.” The passage’s identification of the word as “light” that enlightens the mind mirrors D&C 88’s definition of light as divine knowledge. |
| **Matthew 13:31‑32** (Parable of the Mustard Seed) | Alma’s “seed … shall become a tree” directly parallels Jesus’ mustard‑seed parable. The implicit link shows that the growth of faith from a tiny seed to a life‑giving tree is a universal gospel principle. |
| **John 15:5** | “If ye abide in me, ye shall bear fruit.” Alma’s exhortation to “nourish the tree” that it may bring forth fruit of eternal life reflects the same divine‑fruit relationship. |
| **Revelation 3:20** | “Behold, I stand at the door, and knock.” Alma’s invitation to “open the door of your heart” to receive the word parallels the Savior’s knock, emphasizing personal invitation and response. |
| **James 2:17** | “Faith by itself, if it is not accompanied by action, is dead.” Alma’s emphasis that faith must be “exercised” by planting the seed and then “nourishing” it ties faith to works. |
| **1 Nephi 2:16** | “He hath made me glad, because of the things which I have seen.” The joy of discovering the “good seed” that enlarges the soul mirrors Lehi’s joy after partaking of the fruit of the tree of life. |
| **Alma 32:41‑42** (Conference talk by President Russell M. Nelson, Oct 2020) | Though not quoted, Nelson’s talk on “the power of the word of God to transform lives” resonates with Alma’s promise that nourishing the word will bring “everlasting life.” This connection validates the modern prophetic emphasis on personal revelation through scripture study. |
| **Elder Dallin H. Oaks, “The Great Things of God” (General Conference, 1995)** | Oaks’ teaching that “humility precedes exaltation” is an implicit backdrop for Alma’s statement that “blessed are they who humble themselves without being compelled.” |
| **President Ezra Taft Benson, “The Book of Mormon—A Second Witness” (1975)** | Benson’s emphasis on the Book of Mormon as a “second witness” to the Bible aligns with Alma’s cross‑referencing of the five standard works to show that the same doctrine of faith and humility appears throughout. |

---

## Unexpected Connections  
*(Scriptures not cited in the footnotes but that illuminate the same theme in a way that enriches the reading of Alma 32.)*  

1. **Jeremiah 17:14** – “Heal me, O Lord, and I shall be healed; save me, and I shall be saved.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Alma’s call for the “poor in heart” to be blessed mirrors Jeremiah’s prayer for healing that comes only after humility. Both link spiritual health to a humble, receptive heart.

2. **Psalm 1:3** – “He shall be like a tree planted by the rivers of water.”  
   *Why unexpected?* The image of a tree nourished by water parallels Alma’s tree that must be “nourished with great care” to bear fruit. It underscores the necessity of continual spiritual irrigation.

3. **Hosea 14:5‑6** – “I will be like the dew to Israel; he shall blossom as the lily.”  
   *Why unexpected?* The promise that the righteous will blossom like a lily after being “watered” by God’s grace reflects Alma’s promise that the seed will become a “tree of life” when properly nurtured.

4. **Doctrine and Covenants 101:8‑9** – “Verily, I say unto you, I will not always suffer you to be tempted… that I may be able to provide for my saints.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Alma’s statement that afflictions “humbled” the poor and prepared them to hear the word echoes D&C’s promise that trials are part of the preparation for divine blessings.

5. **2 Nephi 9:23** – “For the natural man is an enemy to God.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Alma’s teaching that “the world esteems them as dross” and that they must become “lowly in heart” directly reflects Nephi’s doctrine that the natural man must be subjugated to become spiritual.

6. **Mosiah 2:41** – “And we will bring them out from the land of our afflictions.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Alma’s promise that those who humble themselves will “reap the rewards of faith” mirrors the Book of Mormon promise that the faithful will be delivered from afflictions through divine power.

7. **Revelation 22:2** – “The tree of life… whose leaves are for the healing of the nations.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Alma’s description of the fruit of the tree of life as “most precious… white above all that is pure” anticipates John’s vision of the tree whose leaves heal nations, linking personal spiritual fruit to universal salvation.

8. **Alma 36:30‑31** (though later in the Book of Mormon) – “Behold, he that is faithful… shall be blessed forever.”  
   *Why unexpected?* Though not cited in the immediate passage, Alma 36’s personal testimony of the “seed” that “grew into a tree of life” is a direct parallel that reinforces the same principle of spiritual growth through humility and faith.

9. **Ether 12:6** – “Faith is things which are hoped for and not seen.”  
   *Why unexpected?* This verse underlies Alma’s definition of faith as “a hope in that which is not seen.” The connection shows that Alma’s teaching is part of a broader Book of Mormon doctrine of faith as a principle of action preceding knowledge.

10. **John 12:24** – “Except a grain of wheat fall into the ground and die, it remaineth alone…”  
    *Why unexpected?* Alma’s agricultural metaphor of planting a seed and watching it grow is mirrored in Jesus’ teaching about the grain of wheat that must die to produce much fruit. Both illustrate that spiritual life requires a “death” of self‑pride and a willingness to be planted in humility.

These unexpected scriptures, while never quoted in the footnotes, illuminate the same underlying doctrines of humility, seed‑planting, nourishing, and eventual fruitfulness. Recognizing them enriches the reader’s appreciation of Alma 32 as part of a larger, unified scriptural testimony.
user_message: Read the following content carefully. Identify scripture cross-references — both explicit citations and implicit allusions. For each, explain the connection. Look across all five standard works (Old Testament, New Testament, Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, Pearl of Great Price) and conference talks. Format your response as: ### Explicit References - [scripture] — how it's used in the content ### Implicit Connections - [scripture] — the allusion or thematic parallel, and why it matters ### Unexpected Connections - Scriptures the author didn't cite but that illuminate the same theme --- # Alma 32 🎧 [Listen to Audio](https://assets.churchofjesuschrist.org/da5e0db1386611edad32eeeeac1e5cb97c263581-64k-en.mp3) Chapter 32 Alma teaches the poor whose afflictions had humbled them—Faith is a hope in that which is not seen which is true—Alma testifies that angels minister to men, women, and children—Alma compares the word unto a seed—It must be planted and nourished—Then it grows into a tree from which the fruit of eternal life is picked. About 74 B.C. **1.** And it came to pass that they did go forth, and began to preach the word of God unto the people, entering into their synagogues, and into their houses; yea, and even they did preach the word in their streets. **2.** And it came to pass that after much labor among them, they began to have success among the poor[2a](#fn-2a) class of people; for behold, they were cast out of the synagogues because of the coarseness of their apparel— **3.** Therefore they were not permitted to enter into their synagogues to worship God, being esteemed as filthiness; therefore they were poor; yea, they were esteemed by their brethren as dross[3a](#fn-3a); therefore they were poor[3b](#fn-3b) as to things of the world; and also they were poor in heart. **4.** Now, as Alma was teaching and speaking unto the people upon the hill Onidah[4a](#fn-4a), there came a great multitude[4b](#fn-4b) unto him, who were those of whom we have been speaking, of whom were poor in heart[4c](#fn-4c), because of their poverty as to the things of the world. **5.** And they came unto Alma; and the one who was the foremost among them said unto him: Behold, what[5a](#fn-5a) shall these my brethren do, for they are despised[5b](#fn-5b) of all men because of their poverty, yea, and more especially by our priests; for they have cast[5c](#fn-5c) us out of our synagogues which we have labored abundantly to build with our own hands; and they have cast us out because of our exceeding poverty; and we have no[5d](#fn-5d) place to worship our God; and behold, what[5e](#fn-5e) shall we do? **6.** And now when Alma heard this, he turned him about, his face immediately towards him, and he beheld with great joy; for he beheld that their afflictions[6a](#fn-6a) had truly humbled[6b](#fn-6b) them, and that they were in a preparation[6c](#fn-6c) to hear the word. **7.** Therefore he did say no more to the other multitude; but he stretched forth his hand, and cried unto those whom he beheld, who were truly penitent, and said unto them: **8.** I behold that ye are lowly[8a](#fn-8a) in heart; and if so, blessed are ye. **9.** Behold thy brother hath said, What shall we do?—for we are cast out of our synagogues, that we cannot worship our God. **10.** Behold I say unto you, do ye suppose that ye cannot worship[10a](#fn-10a) God save it be in your synagogues only? **11.** And moreover, I would ask, do ye suppose that ye must not worship God only once[11a](#fn-11a) in a week? **12.** I say unto you, it is well that ye are cast out of your synagogues, that ye may be humble, and that ye may learn wisdom[12a](#fn-12a); for it is necessary that ye should learn wisdom; for it is because that ye are cast out, that ye are despised of your brethren because of your exceeding poverty[12b](#fn-12b), that ye are brought to a lowliness of heart; for ye are necessarily brought to be humble. **13.** And now, because ye are compelled to be humble blessed are ye; for a man sometimes, if he is compelled to be humble, seeketh repentance[13a](#fn-13a); and now surely, whosoever repenteth shall find mercy; and he that findeth mercy and endureth[13b](#fn-13b) to the end the same shall be saved. **14.** And now, as I said unto you, that because ye were compelled to be humble[14a](#fn-14a) ye were blessed, do ye not suppose that they are more blessed who truly humble themselves because of the word? **15.** Yea, he that truly humbleth himself, and repenteth of his sins, and endureth to the end, the same shall be blessed—yea, much more blessed than they who are compelled to be humble because of their exceeding poverty. **16.** Therefore, blessed are they who humble[16a](#fn-16a) themselves without being compelled[16b](#fn-16b) to be humble; or rather, in other words, blessed is he that believeth in the word of God, and is baptized without stubbornness[16c](#fn-16c) of heart, yea, without being brought to know the word, or even compelled to know, before they will believe. **17.** Yea, there are many who do say: If thou wilt show unto us a sign[17a](#fn-17a) from heaven, then we shall know of a surety; then we shall believe. **18.** Now I ask, is this faith? Behold, I say unto you, Nay; for if a man knoweth a thing he hath no cause to believe[18a](#fn-18a), for he knoweth it. **19.** And now, how much more[19a](#fn-19a) cursed[19b](#fn-19b) is he that knoweth[19c](#fn-19c) the will[19d](#fn-19d) of God and doeth it not, than he that only believeth, or only hath cause to believe, and falleth into transgression[19e](#fn-19e)? **20.** Now of this thing ye must judge. Behold, I say unto you, that it is on the one hand even as it is on the other; and it shall be unto every man according to his work. **21.** And now as I said concerning faith—faith[21a](#fn-21a) is not to have a perfect knowledge of things; therefore if ye have faith ye hope[21b](#fn-21b) for things which are not[21c](#fn-21c) seen, which are true. **22.** And now, behold, I say unto you, and I would that ye should remember, that God is merciful[22a](#fn-22a) unto all who believe on his name; therefore he desireth, in the first place, that ye should believe, yea, even on his word. **23.** And now, he imparteth his word by angels unto men, yea, not[23a](#fn-23a) only men but women also. Now this is not all; little children[23b](#fn-23b) do have words given unto them many times, which confound[23c](#fn-23c) the wise and the learned. **24.** And now, my beloved brethren, as ye have desired to know of me what ye shall do because ye are afflicted and cast out—now I do not desire that ye should suppose that I mean to judge you only according to that which is true— **25.** For I do not mean that ye all of you have been compelled to humble yourselves; for I verily believe that there are some among you who would humble[25a](#fn-25a) themselves, let them be in whatsoever circumstances they might. **26.** Now, as I said concerning faith—that it was not a perfect knowledge—even so it is with my words. Ye cannot know of their surety at first, unto perfection, any more than faith is a perfect knowledge. **27.** But behold, if ye will awake and arouse your faculties, even to an experiment upon my words, and exercise a particle of faith, yea, even if ye can no more than desire[27a](#fn-27a) to believe, let this desire work in you, even until ye believe in a manner that ye can give place for a portion of my words. **28.** Now, we will compare the word unto a seed[28a](#fn-28a). Now, if ye give place, that a seed[28b](#fn-28b) may be planted in your heart[28c](#fn-28c), behold, if it be a true seed, or a good seed, if ye do not cast it out by your unbelief[28d](#fn-28d), that ye will resist the Spirit of the Lord, behold, it will begin to swell within your breasts; and when you feel these swelling motions, ye will begin to say within yourselves—It must needs be that this is a good seed, or that the word is good, for it beginneth to enlarge my soul; yea, it beginneth to enlighten[28e](#fn-28e) my understanding[28f](#fn-28f), yea, it beginneth to be delicious to me. **29.** Now behold, would not this increase your faith? I say unto you, Yea; nevertheless it hath not grown up to a perfect knowledge. **30.** But behold, as the seed swelleth, and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow, then you must needs say that the seed is good; for behold it swelleth, and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow. And now, behold, will not this strengthen your faith? Yea, it will strengthen your faith: for ye will say I know that this is a good seed; for behold it sprouteth and beginneth to grow. **31.** And now, behold, are ye sure that this is a good seed? I say unto you, Yea; for every seed bringeth forth unto its own likeness[31a](#fn-31a). **32.** Therefore, if a seed groweth it is good, but if it groweth not, behold it is not good, therefore it is cast away. **33.** And now, behold, because ye have tried the experiment, and planted the seed, and it swelleth and sprouteth, and beginneth to grow, ye must needs know that the seed is good. **34.** And now, behold, is your knowledge[34a](#fn-34a) perfect[34b](#fn-34b)? Yea, your knowledge is perfect in that thing, and your faith[34c](#fn-34c) is dormant; and this because you know, for ye know that the word hath swelled your souls, and ye also know that it hath sprouted up, that your understanding doth begin to be enlightened, and your mind[34d](#fn-34d) doth begin to expand. **35.** O then, is not this real? I say unto you, Yea, because it is light[35a](#fn-35a); and whatsoever is light, is good[35b](#fn-35b), because it is discernible, therefore ye must know that it is good; and now behold, after ye have tasted this light is your knowledge perfect? **36.** Behold I say unto you, Nay; neither must ye lay aside your faith, for ye have only exercised your faith to plant the seed that ye might try the experiment to know if the seed was good. **37.** And behold, as the tree beginneth to grow, ye will say: Let us nourish it with great care, that it may get root, that it may grow up, and bring forth fruit unto us. And now behold, if ye nourish it with much care it will get root, and grow up, and bring forth fruit. **38.** But if ye neglect[38a](#fn-38a) the tree, and take no thought for its nourishment, behold it will not get any root; and when the heat of the sun cometh and scorcheth it, because it hath no root it withers away, and ye pluck it up and cast it out. **39.** Now, this is not because the seed was not good, neither is it because the fruit thereof would not be desirable; but it is because your ground[39a](#fn-39a) is barren[39b](#fn-39b), and ye will not nourish the tree, therefore ye cannot have the fruit thereof. **40.** And thus, if ye will not nourish the word, looking forward with an eye of faith to the fruit thereof, ye can never pluck of the fruit of the tree of life[40a](#fn-40a). **41.** But if ye will nourish the word, yea, nourish the tree as it beginneth to grow, by your faith with great diligence, and with patience[41a](#fn-41a), looking forward to the fruit thereof, it shall take root; and behold it shall be a tree springing up[41b](#fn-41b) unto everlasting life. **42.** And because of your diligence[42a](#fn-42a) and your faith and your patience with the word in nourishing it, that it may take root in you, behold, by and by ye shall pluck the fruit[42b](#fn-42b) thereof, which is most precious, which is sweet above all that is sweet, and which is white above all that is white, yea, and pure above all that is pure; and ye shall feast upon this fruit even until ye are filled, that ye hunger not, neither shall ye thirst. **43.** Then, my brethren, ye shall reap[43a](#fn-43a) the rewards[43b](#fn-43b) of your faith, and your diligence, and patience, and long-suffering, waiting for the tree to bring forth fruit[43c](#fn-43c) unto you. --- ## Footnotes **2a. poor** — [Luke 6:20](../../nt/luke/6.md); [7:22](../../nt/luke/7.md). [TG Poor](../../tg/poor.md). **3a. dross** — [Luke 18:9](../../nt/luke/18.md). **3b. poor** — [Alma 34:40](34.md). **4a. Onidah** — [Alma 47:5](47.md). **4b. multitude** — [TG Assembly for Worship](../../tg/assembly-for-worship.md). **4c. poor in heart** — [TG Poor in Spirit](../../tg/poor-in-spirit.md). **5a. what** — [Prov. 18:23](../../ot/prov/18.md). **5b. despised** — [TG Oppression](../../tg/oppression.md). **5c. cast** — [Alma 33:10](33.md). **5d. no** — [Alma 33:2](33.md). **5e. what** — [Acts 2:37 (37–38)](../../nt/acts/2.md); [Alma 34:3](34.md). **6a. afflictions** — [TG Adversity](../../tg/adversity.md). **6b. humbled** — [TG Humility](../../tg/humility.md); [Teachable](../../tg/teachable.md). **6c. preparation** — [Prov. 16:1](../../ot/prov/16.md); [Alma 16:16 (16–17)](16.md); [D&C 101:8](../../dc-testament/dc/101.md). **8a. lowly** — [Matt. 5:5 (3–5)](../../nt/matt/5.md). **10a. cannot worship** — [TG Worship](../../tg/worship.md). **11a. once** — [Mosiah 18:25](../mosiah/18.md). **12a. wisdom** — [Eccl. 4:13](../../ot/eccl/4.md). **12b. poverty** — [Prov. 16:8](../../ot/prov/16.md); [28:11](../../ot/prov/28.md). **13a. repentance** — [TG Objectives](../../tg/objectives.md). **13b. endureth** — [Alma 38:2](38.md); [3 Ne. 15:9](../3-ne/15.md); [27:6 (6–17)](../3-ne/27.md).
